coredata.dc:title: State-of-charge estimation of IoT devices using genetic algorithm-optimized long short-term memory networks under real-world conditions

coredata.dc:description: Accurate State of Charge (SOC) estimation is crucial for remaining energy estimation and remaining useful life prediction. While in application scenarios such as Electric Vehicles (EVs), accurate SOC estimation can help to plan journeys, reducing range anxiety; for WSN and IoT devices on the other hand, accurate SOC estimation may result in prolonged device lifetime. In this regard, most of the techniques consider battery terminal voltage, capacity, and even EMF to estimate the SOC, but mostly ignore the real-world conditions. To address this gap, a controlled experimental corpus covering three battery chemistries (Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH), three constant discharge currents (30, 50, and 100 mA), and five ambient temperatures (5°C, 15°C, 25°C, 35°C, and 45°C) was assembled. Each condition was repeated three times, where cycles with anomalies were excluded to quantify reproducibility. A Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model tuned with a Genetic Algorithm (GA) was trained per chemistry using five-fold cross-validation, with optimization targeting hyperparameters (including layer depth, hidden units, dropout, learning rate, and batch size).The proposed model (LSTM-GA) provided an average low Mean Absolute Value (MAE) of 0.349, 0.390, and 0.412 for Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH battery chemistry, respectively. The proposed technique also offers a complete adaptive, scalable code pipeline for model training and deployment. Finally, training cost and inference latency were quantified on NVIDIA Titan V and RTX 4070 GPUs (running TensorFlow 2.20 with CUDA 13), with latencies shown to be appropriate for embedded platform deployment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate State of Charge (SOC) estimation is crucial for remaining energy estimation and remaining useful life prediction. While in application scenarios such as Electric Vehicles (EVs), accurate SOC estimation can help to plan journeys, reducing range anxiety; for WSN and IoT devices on the other hand, accurate SOC estimation may result in prolonged device lifetime. In this regard, most of the techniques consider battery terminal voltage, capacity, and even EMF to estimate the SOC, but mostly ignore the real-world conditions. To address this gap, a controlled experimental corpus covering three battery chemistries (Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH), three constant discharge currents (30, 50, and 100 mA), and five ambient temperatures (5°C, 15°C, 25°C, 35°C, and 45°C) was assembled. Each condition was repeated three times, where cycles with anomalies were excluded to quantify reproducibility. A Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model tuned with a Genetic Algorithm (GA) was trained per chemistry using five-fold cross-validation, with optimization targeting hyperparameters (including layer depth, hidden units, dropout, learning rate, and batch size).The proposed model (LSTM-GA) provided an average low Mean Absolute Value (MAE) of 0.349, 0.390, and 0.412 for Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH battery chemistry, respectively. The proposed technique also offers a complete adaptive, scalable code pipeline for model training and deployment. Finally, training cost and inference latency were quantified on NVIDIA Titan V and RTX 4070 GPUs (running TensorFlow 2.20 with CUDA 13), with latencies shown to be appropriate for embedded platform deployment.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past two decades, WSN and IoT devices have witnessed exponential adoption rates, with billions of these devices deployed for numerous tasks, including sensing, actuation, and monitoring. Most of these applications involve low-cost environment monitoring applications, however, these devices are also deployed in mission-critical applications due to their smaller form factor and reduced unit cost. With the fast-paced technological advancements in the semiconductor and electronics industry, the cost, size, and capabilities of these devices have significantly increased. For instance, in the early 2000s, the WSN devices (a precursor to IoT devices) had limited memory, compute, and networking resources, whereas modern IoT edge devices have higher computation, memory, and communication capabilities that support larger bandwidths, enabling them to even run Machine Learning (ML) models directly on the edge. However, one stringent requirement still challenges their operational efficiency, which is limited by scarce on-board energy typically powered by batteries. Although several energy harvesting techniques have been proposed, due to the WSN and IoT node deployment nature, the environment, and operational limitations, these energy conservation and generation techniques are mostly not feasible. Additionally, in most of the application scenarios, the maintenance costs for these devices are larger than the actual cost. Therefore, the end of available battery energy typically dictates the end of a WSN node’s operation; preferably it is replaced by a new node. Therefore, energy estimation and monitoring are crucial to design efficient algorithms and optimization strategies for the entire technology stack to extend the device lifetime. This is typically established by estimating the current State of Charge (SOC) of the batteries, which directly translates into the readily available energy that could be utilized by the device. There are numerous SOC estimation techniques available that vary in accuracy, complexity, computational requirements, and application scenarios. These include electrochemical methods, impedance analysis methods, Ampere-hour (Ah) method, EMF estimation, and ML-based methods, to name a few. Recent studies indicate significant advancements in SOC estimation utilizing deep recurrent architectures, especially LSTM and its bidirectional variants, on electric vehicle-scale datasets and dynamic driving cycles (Chemali et al., 2017; Chong et al., 2020). Research indicates that sequence models more effectively capture hysteresis and temperature-dependent dynamics compared to static regressors or shallow networks. Most evaluations, however, focus on high-current or rapidly varying profiles, while the hyperparameter search procedures are frequently under-specified, which impedes reproducibility (Zhan et al., 2022). Similarly, hybrid estimators that integrate LSTM with Bayesian filters have demonstrated additional improvements in dynamic operations (Carrera et al., 2025). However, their validation primarily focuses on electric vehicle contexts rather than the low-current, intermittently active conditions characteristic of IoT devices. Since WSN and IoT devices have limited computational and energy requirements, SOC estimation at the expense of additional energy consumption is not ideal. Furthermore, since these devices are deployed in harsh environments and operate on small currents, the impact of varying device loads (current), and environment conditions (such as temperature) can have compound impacts on energy management.

Considering these limitations, WSN and IoT devices typically demand lightweight algorithms for computationally efficient, adaptive, and variable operations. In this regard, Ah-based techniques typically dominate where battery discharge patterns are analyzed over a continuous discharge cycle. However, such schemes must rigorously consider the impact of load and environmental conditions on various battery chemistries to be able to model the SOC accurately. Most of the proposed techniques either focus on a certain type of battery chemistry or on a fixed set of load and discharge modes that fail to capture the impact on other parameters as mentioned above. Furthermore, since most of these devices operate on a single battery, it is impossible to measure and validate the terminal voltages in the field. Therefore, SOC is typically measured by estimating a specific initial charge state, which is used to calculate energy consumption in the preceding states as the device operates. However, since batteries are electrochemical nonlinear devices, charging, rest period, and rate of discharge (C-rate) impact the available energy calculations. Furthermore, these variables can also impact battery’s State of Health (SOH) which may result in energy retention and reaching equilibrium states at specific SOC levels.

Therefore, this research presents a holistic approach to investigate the impact of both the operational and environmental parameters on various battery chemistries. In this regard, a detailed set of experiments were conducted on various battery chemistries (including Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH), where battery preparation, relaxation, and pre-conditioning framework (Ali et al., 2022a) is closely followed so that the batteries reach optimum equilibrium states. Battery SOC and terminal voltages were computed for each discharge cycle (continuous load, continuous discharge) at a range of temperature values (5°C, 15°C, 25°C, 35°C, 45°C) that are typically encountered by indoor as well as outdoor installations of WSN devices. This information (terminal voltage, SOC, load current, and actual current consumed) was then used to design ML models that take into consideration all these parameters to remove the above-mentioned shortcomings. In addition, a time series based on moving average is calculated only for the states where a change in (terminal voltage and current consumed) is observed, thus removing noise and variations induced due to device sleep states. Finally, Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTM) based models with KTuner hyperparameter tuning and Genetic Algorithm (GA) based tuning for each battery chemistry is trained and validated for an extensive dataset (at the above-mentioned temperature ranges) for model precision and accuracy. Battery discharge patterns show extended plateaus where battery chemistry-specific hysteresis and temporal variations are observed. This effect is at its peak, especially in the flat region with similar voltages across different SOC states. LSTM encodes these dependencies via gated memory and has shown strong SOC performance on sequential data, particularly in plateau regions. Furthermore, since the battery discharge behavior is non-linear, the optimized parameter search space is non-convex. Therefore, GA based parameter optimization ensures reduced search bias and consistently achieves lower-loss regions for all battery chemistries. The results show that reduced MAE values of 0.349, 0.390, and 0.412 were achieved for Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH (operating at above-mentioned environmental conditions) battery chemistry, respectively.

The main contributions of this paper are as follows:
•
A reproducible experimental dataset spanning 3 battery chemistries (Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH), 3 constant discharge currents (30, 50, and 100 mA), and 5 temperatures (5°C, 15°C, 25°C, 35°C, and 45°C) yields a total of 45 experimental conditions plus a second replication set to average out run-to-run variability.
•
Time-series based on moving average discharge curves processing (terminal voltage and current) to eliminate anomalous, duplicate, and overlapping values.
•
A detailed LSTM-GA tuning architecture, with reproducible architecture, code, and optimization configurations.
•
A head-to-head evaluation of GA vs. KTuner in LSTM optimization reporting both accuracy (MAE, RMSE, and R2 with standard deviation across every fold/repeat) and computational cost (training time per model, total GA evaluations, and inference latency on Titan V and RTX 4070 GPUs).

The article provides practical guidance for edge deployment requirements, including model reduction, quantization, and packaging directions for embedded platforms. The remainder of the article is organized as follows: 2 presents an extensive literature review of existing SOC estimation techniques specifically for low-power electronic devices. 3 describes the data collection, processing, design, and optimization of LSTM architecture and GA-based tuning. Whereas the experimental results and discussions are presented in 4. Finally, the article concludes by summarizing the findings, notable achievements, and outlining future steps.

Accurate battery state of charge (SOC) estimation is crucial to identify the remaining useful energy (RUL) in the batteries to power the electronic devices. Most of the high-power applications, such as EVs, focus on additional factors such as SOH due to numerous charge and discharge cycles over the entire life of these electrochemical devices. However, low-power electronic applications, specifically WSN and IoT applications with remote deployment scenarios, do not consider battery health, as the depletion of battery energy usually signifies the end of the device lifetime. Furthermore, although environmental and operational factors have a significant impact on high-power electronics, several techniques are integrated to prepare batteries for extreme weather and high discharge conditions. Additionally, since these devices can be charged, the impact mostly results in gradual degradation. However, in typical WSN and IoT applications, these conditions may result in complete or intermittent failures. Therefore, several SOC estimation techniques (although mathematically similar) were proposed with varying results in WSN and IoT applications. One of the most common techniques in this regard is based on the Coulomb counting method, where the initial battery energy is calculated and then integrated over time to incorporate the discharge currents until the batteries reach cut-off voltages. Although the easiest method to implement, these techniques suffer from cumulative errors due to battery self-discharge, varying load conditions, temperature drifts, and varying discharge patterns, due to which battery recovery periods are mostly neglected. Several enhanced variants of Coulomb Counting based on Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) recalibration and adaptive loss compensation methods attempt to reduce these errors (Ali et al., 2022b) (Ng et al., 2009). In this regard, Martin et al., proposed piecewise linear mapping by integrating the initial SOC, OCV, and depth of discharge (DOD) to enhance SOC estimation which reduced errors down to 2 % for Li-Ion batteries (Martin Murnane, 2023) Along the same lines, the study in (Mohammadi, 2022) also investigated Li-Ion battery uncertainties within an enhanced mathematical model that reports 0.3 % estimation errors. Although the model accounted for battery nonlinearities and the impact of temperature, it is mostly suited towards EV applications and may not account for over SOC reporting at sub-zero temperatures. However, temperature drifts can be modelled for moderate environments for IoT nodes, reducing the thermal drift effect, which results in increasing estimation accuracies of 1.5 %, as discussed by (Martin Murnane, 2023; Chaoui and Mandalapu, 2017). Furthermore, several methods propose hybrid implementations based on SOH-coupled systems. Lanubile et al., proposed a dynamically adjusted battery capacity fading method that couples SOH estimation for enhanced SOC estimation, reporting sustained accuracies of over 98 % (Lanubile et al., 2024). Although numerous models and enhanced techniques were provided to adjust environmental factors such as thermal drifts, no direct comparison was established for WSN and IoT applications that may require external temperature sensors to report and validate thermal effects. Furthermore, with duty-cycled low-current discharge, these methods are introduced with transient current spikes requiring additional hardware and terminal voltage testing, which is not possible in this application scenario.

Another important technique that takes into consideration these factors by mathematically modelling the transients relies on Kalman Filters (KF) and their variants. KF based methods, specifically Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) and Adaptive EKF improve model accuracy by mapping battery nonlinearities (Ren and Du, 2023). In this regard, Siong Chin et al., investigated the effect of ambient and cell temperatures on Li-Iron Phosphate batteries for typical EV applications using EKF (Chin et al., 2018). The model provided consistent accuracy over a range of temperatures. Although the research reported reduced RMSEs at 5 °C, 15 °C, and 45 °C, the limited information on temperature chambers at all temperatures could not be validated. Along the same lines, Xu et al., presented a second-order temperature-dependent model for Li-Ion batteries that also integrates the Coulomb counting technique (Xu et al., 2019). The model reported over 98 % accuracy for all temperature values; however, on-device model validation was not established. Similarly, Wenyuan et al., proposed a hybrid approach based on the Adaptive EKF (AEKF) method by using feed forward neural networks (FFNN) for RC parameter estimation for accurate temperature changes. This hybrid approach provided high accuracy over a range of temperatures, including sub-zero temperatures (Bai et al., 2022). Along the same lines, Haris et al., proposed an energy-efficient SOC estimation method using filtering methods specifically for embedded platforms (Turkmanović et al., 2024). It was reported that the model provided 30 % reduced energy consumption as compared to other higher-order models, however, while conserving energy, the model suffered a slight (1–2 %) accuracy loss. Although this could translate to very large estimation inaccuracy towards duty-cycled devices, with lesser energy consumption of the model, these inaccuracies could balance each other. Thermal adaptation into models can increase the estimation accuracy; however, with offline validation, computational complexity, as well as the increased energy consumption, these methods may not be directly suitable for embedded platform deployment.

The equivalent circuit models (ECM), sometimes referred to as RC models, utilize traditional electrical circuit elements (such as capacitors, resistors, and voltage sources) to conceive a network that can hold and store charge, thus replicating battery storage behavior. Most of these models focus on external battery parameters, whereas higher-order complex models integrate battery intrinsic properties (He et al., 2011, 2012). The voltage sources are mostly used to replicate battery states in their equilibrium state, whereas RC elements are used to characterize the energy flow based on operational characteristics. Although several ECM techniques and their variants have been proposed, mostly Rint, Thevenin, PNGV, and GNL-based ECM methods are reported and are typically used to model EV applications. Since the Rint model only focuses on an ideal voltage source and internal resistance, it only accounts for ideal states and fewer parameters, thus limiting its adaptability and accuracy, despite its simpler structure (Wang et al., 2023; Nejad et al., 2016). Therefore, little has been reported on this model for actual application scenarios. Along the same lines, Thevenin models use RC circuit elements to model the effects of battery polarization characteristics, specifically during charging and heavy discharging processes. Several improved models were proposed to enhance the model accuracy for various charge and discharge profiles. In this regard, Zhao et al. proposed a dual polarization-based Thevenin model that dynamically adjusts polarization resistance based on the load current (Zhao et al., 2017). Similarly, in (Zhang et al., 2018; He et al., 2021) the authors proposed variable-order Thevenin models that account for battery SOC and temperature variations, thus allowing for better estimation at dynamic environmental and load conditions. The PNGV model utilizes capacitors in series with the Thevenin model to represent changes in OCV induced by load currents. However, it is only adapted to HPPC or equivalent pulses for the discharge profile (Geng et al., 2022). Similarly, the GNL model extends the PNGV model by also considering the impact of batteries’ self-discharging. Although this model accurately maps battery internal chemistry and operational characteristics, the application and battery-specific parameter identification and tuning become a computationally complex process, thus limiting its application for low-compute power embedded platforms (Meng et al., 2018).

Data-driven techniques excel at mapping battery behaviors without deeply investigating the background processes, intrinsic battery properties, and complex chemical reactions that are taking place. However, the quality of the data for battery behavior, internal and external factors, and operating conditions directly translates into model accuracy and adaptability (Liu et al., 2019; Hossain Lipu et al., 2020). Furthermore, insufficient data may often result in under or overfitting issues, whereas noise removal data pre-processing techniques often result in misinterpretation during various phases of the battery cycle (Chen et al., 2018). Several data-driven SOC estimation techniques have been discussed in the literature, ranging from classical regression, probabilistic, and rule-based techniques to neural networks and advanced recurrent neural networks (RNNs) techniques. However, since the discharge profiles in this research based on environmental and operational factors are time-series based, LSTM-based RNNs are discussed here to link the current literature with the proposed technique. Traditional RNNs suffer from the vanishing gradients problem; therefore, LSTMs with their memory units are a viable option to map all battery parameters, especially for the linear regions where little to no variations in battery voltages are present. A stacked LSTM architecture was proposed in (Yang et al., 2019) that captures non-linear relationships among current, battery voltage, SOC, and temperatures, specifically for Lithium-Iron Phosphate (LiFePO) batteries. The model offered over 98 % accuracy for various operating conditions for EVs with response times in millisecond range; however, it required higher computational power and may not be suitable for embedded platforms. Along the same lines, in (Bian et al., 2020), Bian et al., trained a bidirectional LSTM network for the EV drive cycle dataset (Kollmeyer et al., 2020), to map dynamic nonlinear relationships of the battery. In this study, highly accurate SOC estimation of over 98.7 % was achieved for various Li-Ion based battery chemistries operating at various temperatures. Along the same lines, the researchers in (Lu et al., 2025) investigated Particle Swamp Optimization (PSO) application to control Kalman gain for LiFePO4 batteries, resulting in a 27.64 % reduced MAE score in the OCV plateau region. Shulin et al., (Liu et al., 2022) proposed an enhanced 2RC model to report SOC and SOH estimation with estimation errors less than 0.08 %. Another SOC model enhancement concept was presented in (Ji et al., 2025), where a digital twin battery model based on the ECM and neural network approach reported consistent SOC estimating accuracy across all regions. Similarly, in (Chemali et al., 2017), Chemali et al., investigated the LSTM model generalization capabilities with various cycle depths at several SOC levels and temperatures. The study reported a 0.673 % Mean Absolute Error (MAE) at ambient temperature with slight variations at other temperatures. Although these techniques investigated various discharge depths and temperatures as external factors, the impact of various load conditions, rapid temperature changes, and the use of battery chemistries, including and other than Li-Ion, were not investigated. Furthermore, the model response times were reported on high compute power platforms typically available for EV applications. These factors limit the adaptability and scalability of these techniques towards low-compute power embedded platforms and therefore require a more robust approach to overcome these challenges, as proposed in this research and detailed in the following sections. Table 1 summarizes various metrics, adaptability, and challenges associated with SOC estimation techniques discussed in this section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery preconditioning, relaxation, and discharge rates
ce:section.ce:para: Although a range of battery chemistries are available, battery type, shape, energy density, and cost are some of the most critical factors in their selection. These parameters also greatly affect the internal chemical reaction dynamics and thus the variations in performance based on the operational and environmental factors. In most of the applications, such as EVs and smart grids, the battery energy density, composition, and cycle count help to identify its viability and thus RUL. On the other hand, for WSN and IoT applications, the end of a charge cycle typically identifies RUL and therefore greatly simplifies the selection process.

However, two dominating factors that influence the selection criteria remain, including the cost and the concentration of energy throughout the discharge profile. These factors impact the techno-economic viability for various WSN and IoT application deployments. Although Alkaline-based cells have been used for a long time for low-power electronics, the available energy greatly varies based on operational conditions; therefore, they were not considered in this research. Table 2 lists the battery used in this research, whereas the overall process flow (from battery conditioning to data collection, to model development and optimization) is presented in Fig. 1.

Li-Ion based batteries operate at or above 3.6 V, thus directly operational for WSN and IoT applications. However, Ni-MH cells have a lower voltage; therefore, a cell pack of 3 was designed to achieve similar voltage levels. It is important to mention that the average energy per cell pack is influenced by the weakest cell in the pack; therefore, careful selection of cells was needed during pre-conditioning stages. These tests were important to remove any passivation or memory effects, especially for the new cells. Furthermore, cell preconditioning enabled the achievement of the optimum equilibrium state that guarantees the maximum rated energy per cell. In this regard, a series of five consecutive charge and discharge current cycles (continuous current mode) was used to stabilize the battery capacity. Additionally, a relaxation period of 6 h was chosen to allow the internal chemical reactions to reach equilibrium for each battery, as detailed in (Ali et al., 2022a). The pre-conditioning followed by an appropriate relaxation period helps to stabilize the battery’s internal chemical reactions, resulting in smaller voltage fluctuations and achieving optimum capacity as compared to the rated capacity.

EVs, smart grids, and other high-power applications require various discharge current techniques ranging from continuous current and high rate to constant power discharge, where battery SOC, DOD, recovery effects, and SOH are constantly monitored for the specific battery chemistry used. However, for typical WSN and IoT applications (without energy harvesting capabilities), since most of these devices are duty-cycled and preferred to remain in a sleep state for energy conservation, the (sleep/wake), sense, and communication patterns require intermittent and variable discharge currents, where constant current is drawn in each state (Tektronix, 2021). Additionally, since the drawn currents were very low, the impact on chemical reactions, internal heating, and stabilization is minimal, and therefore the recovery period between various cycles is negligibly small. In this research, the Texas Instrument CC1352 R2 development platform was used to record the battery discharge measurement, as well as to be used as a WSN node. This development platform integrates CC2420 radio transceivers operating on the IEEE 802.15.4 protocol, typically used in WSN applications. The platform operates at low power for each state; however, considering adaptive communication power levels, continuous sensor controller current consumption, peripheral interfaces, and typical sensors installed on a WSN node may increase the average current consumption (Instrument, 2019). Table 3 details current consumption in these states, along with the average chosen C-rate selected to represent these states during data collection.

It is important to mention that the MCU and radio sleep states were not considered, since the impact of these ultra-low power currents (µA) is marginal. Additionally, only the most important and highest current-consuming states of the MCU were considered so that the corresponding discharge currents could be recorded for each battery chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery discharge experiment setup, calibration, data collection, and pre-processing
ce:section.ce:para: Three battery chemistries (Li-Ion, Li-Po, and Ni-MH), as described in Table 2, operating in constant current discharge mode (30 mA, 50 mA, and 100 mA), as detailed in Table 3, and at environmental thermal conditions (5 °C, 15 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C, and 45 °C) were chosen to investigate the effect of these parameters on the above-mentioned battery types. This resulted in a total of (3x3x5 = 45) experiments to collect the battery parameters over the entire discharge profiles. It is also important to mention that all batteries underwent pre-conditioning and relaxation periods, as mentioned in the previous section.

All experiments were performed in a programmable thermal chamber, which was stabilized at each temperature setpoint for a minimum of 30 min prior to testing. Batteries were placed in a thermal chamber for 4 h before each test to ensure thermal equilibrium. Cell-surface temperature was monitored with an RSPro RS-1720 probe (RSPro, 2018) (-20 °C to +100 °C: ±0.1 K ± 1 digit; -70 °C to +250 °C: ±0.2 % of measured value ±2 digits). Constant-current discharges were programmed using a B&K Precision BK8600 programmable load (BKPrecision, 2022), and measured values were logged at 1 min intervals, as illustrated in Fig. 2. Furthermore, cycles with instrumentation anomalies (e.g., probe disconnection, unexpected load drop) were excluded and documented. To reduce run-to-run variability, each discharge condition was repeated three times as independent replications. Replicates were performed after full cell recharging, pre-conditioning, and re-soaking at the target chamber temperature to ensure thermal and electrochemical equilibrium. For each condition, results are reported as the mean of the three replications, with the associated standard deviation representing variability across runs, as shown in Fig. 4. This replication strategy ensured that transient fluctuations due to instrumentation noise, contact resistance, or cycle-to-cycle cell variability were averaged out, thereby increasing the robustness of both the dataset and the subsequent model evaluation.

The use of at least three independent replications per condition is consistent with recommendations in battery testing standards and recent experimental studies, where cycle-to-cycle variability and sensor noise are known to introduce systematic error if single runs are used for model training and evaluation. Replication enables quantification of within-condition variability, facilitates propagation of uncertainty into model metrics, and reduces the likelihood that anomalous runs disproportionately influence results. Taylor et al (Taylor et al., 2019). showed that even under controlled laboratory protocols, repeated Li-ion discharge experiments can show siginifacnt differences because of sensor calibration drift and thermal gradients. Following this principle, each discharge condition in the present study was repeated three times, with results reported as mean standard deviation across these replications. Finally, for each experimental condition, SOC error metrics, including Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and coefficient of determination (R²), were calculated and reported across folds/iterations.

The electronic load was programmed to record current and voltage measurements with a 1-minute interval difference, which were then used to calculate SOC using the Ah method. Furthermore, the Texas Instrument CC1352 MCU was used to control time synchronization and temperature measurements from the thermal chamber as well as the battery surface. These temperature values (Tambient, Tsurface, Tchamber) were time synchronized and appended with the battery discharge readings (SOC, Load Current, Terminal voltage) for all experiment cycles. The recorded discharge behaviors from these experiments are shown in Fig. 3.

Since the environmental and operational conditions were controlled and no other external factors were observed, therefore, unlike (Ali et al., 2022b) no augmented drive cycles were required to report the impact of a sudden change (environmental or operational) that may not have any significant impact on the battery capacity. Furthermore, it is important to mention that all incomplete discharge cycle datasets (due to battery or equipment failure) were discarded. Additionally, data normalization and standardization techniques were performed individually on each battery's dataset. In this regard, normalization on the features (including capacity, SOC, load current, and voltage) was performed. However, time-synchronized temperature values were not transformed, since their impact on the parameters is directly reflected in voltage and SOC measurements. As the recorded features exhibited different units and scales, these were therefore normalized in order to avoid bias in prior model training. The Min-Max normalization technique was applied to the capacity and SOC features, scaling the values to a fixed range between 0 and 1 (since the absolute values were fixed and bound by a range, for instance, 0–100 % in case of SOC). This preserved the distribution of the original data while bringing all values into the same scale. However, due to the battery non-linearity, battery discharge cycles do not typically follow Gaussian distribution; therefore, linear scaling techniques were avoided since they could flatten the battery discharge plateaus. These distribution curves highlighting discharge boundaries and plateaus (corresponding to each battery type at 30 mA load) are presented in Fig. 4.

In this regard, the Yeo-Johnson power transform (as detailed in Eq. 1) was used, for battery voltages and currents, since the fitting function is data-driven and more accurately fits the trends for all such values for each battery type. Power transformation normalizes the set of input variables (x) and iterates the scaling function ( λ ), solving for both convex and concave values accordingly.

This normalization process significantly improved the convergence of our machine learning algorithms, especially those relying on distance calculations or gradient descent optimization. However, it was observed that the normalization techniques, particularly min-max scaling, could be sensitive to outliers. However, since the noisy and incomplete data was discarded, these effects were marginal. It is worth mentioning that the power transformation is computationally heavy, but since the data preprocessing, data warehousing, and model development are performed offline, these marginal computational overheads were accepted for improved model accuracy. Since we decided to build different models for each battery type, characterization based on battery types was not required, and therefore these features were not vectorized. This decision was based on the understanding that each battery type behaves differently, especially Ni-MH in comparison to Lithium-based batteries, and a generalized model may not provide accurate results; thus, the datasets were used separately. Since the battery discharge cycle is divided into three zones (exponential, flat, and exponential with rapid discharge), as shown in Fig. 5, this characteristic necessitated temporal mapping to further assist the time-series data, especially near the boundaries of each zone where corresponding voltage values remain similar for a very long period.

It was observed that without this consideration, incorrect normalized mapping between voltage and SOC could occur, greatly impacting the accuracy of SOC estimation (especially near the boundary regions). In order to address this, the rate of change of voltage across capacity was observed near boundary regions using moving average, ensuring it followed the natural gradient of the dataset. This allowed to accurately record the temporal relationships, particularly at the critical transition areas between the discharge regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data partitioning and model evaluation metrics
ce:section.ce:para: The battery dataset was divided in an 80–20 split ratio, allocating 80 % for training and 20 % for testing to avoid overfitting the data. Next, K-fold (5-fold) cross-validation was used for model validation and hyperparameter tuning, which is a crucial phase in the KTuner hyperparameter optimization procedure. The validated set enables KTuner to iterate over various model and parameter configurations by selecting the best fit and minimum loss function model for each battery type. In this regard, the performance metrics against each fold provided an accurate representation of model performance, reducing the likelihood of overly optimistic or pessimistic performance evaluations based on a single split.

The model performance for each battery type was evaluated using R
2 , Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) values. Furthermore, the time and energy consumption for SOC estimation was recorded and contrasted against the technique proposed in (Ali et al., 2022b). Typically, correlation between features measures the similarities between them and is estimated for feature selection, which directly impacts model performance. Whereas metrics such as R
2 or the coefficient of determination report the variance between them. These values are usually reported on a scale between (< −1, 1), where a value closest to 1 represents the best fit, and on the other hand, values close to 0 or negative represent a larger variance between actual and predicted values. Although models with values closer to 0 are not considered, the high degree of negative values results in predicting values away from the mean of the target variables, typically indicating issues with data and training. In essence, these metrics, as given by Eq. 2, were considered to evaluate the overall goodness of fit for a certain model.

Once the goodness of fit was established, it was important to analyze whether the model is underestimating or overestimating the data. MAE reports this behavior by investigating the variance between the forecasted and actual values. As expressed in Eq. 3, MAE provides a straightforward metric that averages the error in between actual and predicted values, where a smaller score is preferable for a good fit. Furthermore, these scores can reach infinity for poorly functioning models with increasing prediction errors.

Although MAE helped to establish a range between prediction errors, it does not report the error progression, which is important to evaluate model accuracy and whether model tuning and optimization could further enhance its accuracy. In order to address this, typically RMSE values were calculated that report the residuals of the errors between actual and estimated values. As MAE is not sensitive to outliers, it may not report larger variations, which in these experiments may occur due to temperature changes or at the borders of battery discharge zones. RMSE, on the other hand, as given in Eq. 4, records these large errors which are helpful in identifying whether the deviation in SOC estimation is due to the data, training, or the model itself, resulting in a higher reliability of prediction. Ideally, both MAE and RMSE values must be low for an accurate, highly reliable model. However, with a smaller MAE and slightly larger RMSE values, it is easier to find outliers, which can be removed by improving data normalization, transformation, and model optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTM) with Genetic Algorithm (GA) optimization for SOC estimation
ce:section.ce:para:
LSTM networks are a type of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) that is capable of automatically learning the patterns in sequential, typically time-series based data. Unlike classical RNNs that suffer from gradient vanishing and gradient explosion problems, LSTMs with their back propagation through time (BPTT) learning unfold the time sequences with shared weights to adjust the errors at the end of each iteration. Therefore, LSTMs can learn the intricate non-linear system dependencies and relationships easily; however, they require a large dataset for training. For every pattern recognition-based learning, the models require a large dataset to fully capture the temporal dependencies by mapping input sequences to output variables. Many of these temporal dependencies in sequential data are historic and directly impact the next set of sequences. LSTMs utilize the internal memory element to retain the historic sequences and only propagate selective weights based on the decision criteria set by their loss functions, thus enabling accurate mapping on application basis. In these experiments, input vectors ( ψ = V
t , I
t
, T
amb
, T
surf ) were used, whereas SOC was the ground truth value. Since there were multiple datasets recorded at various operational and environmental conditions for each battery chemistry, the system mappings can be generalized as (Chemali et al., 2017), given by { ψ
1, SOC1*), ( ψ 2, SOC2*),., ( ψ
N, SOCN*)} where SOCk* is the predicted SOC, ψ
N are the set of input vectors at time step k, and V
t , I
t , T
amb , T
surf are the terminal voltage, current flowing, ambient temperature, and battery surface temperature values, respectively. As mentioned, each subset contained over 8000 time steps; therefore, careful consideration was required as to whether combine the entire dataset for improving training efficiency. However, concatenating these files resulted in over 700,000 time steps each, where overlapping timestamps may pose a threat to losing the temporal dependencies of a particular experiment condition. Therefore, the unrolling timestep technique was used, where a subset of these sequences for each battery was fed to train the models, thus resulting in a specific model for each battery type, as illustrated in Fig. 6.

Although the common trend with larger datasets is to build and optimize a single model that adapts to the problem at hand. However, typical WSN and IoT applications have concrete architecture and operational requirements; therefore, application-specific models were tuned. For instance, the battery types, cut-off voltages, and operating currents are all decided; therefore, it is least likely that battery chemistries can be changed for edge devices. Therefore, independent models per battery chemistry were trained, where the specific subset of corresponding input vectors were used to initially train the models, thus serving as a benchmark. Next, once the loss function was computed and evaluated, a generalized model for the entire sequence of the dataset was trained to estimate SOC for all battery types. In order to develop the SOC estimation model, the TensorFlow framework was used for model training and inference on two hardware platforms with NVIDIA’s Titan V and RTX4070 Graphical Processing Units (GPUs). Although designed for general-purpose graphics and gaming applications, these GPUs provide tensor cores that enable rapid prototyping to keep a simpler architecture for relative measurements, comparison, and reproducibility. The LSTM network, as described and illustrated in Fig. 6, unfolds the training subset sequence where the LSTM architecture learns and maps the relationships using the following composite functions given in Eqs. 5–9.

The forget, input, cell state (long-term memory), and output gates are represented by f, i, c, and o, for each set of iterations k, where a sigmoid σ is calculated by multiplying the network weight W, and a bias factor b of for each gate provides flexibility in fitting the network closest to the data. For every iteration, the model computes the SOC, which is then measured against the (k + 1) th SOC to compute the disparity between these values. Together, these differences between these values were computed to calculate the overall loss function for each pass and subsequently the overall fit function of the model as given by the equation below.

Where α corresponds to battery aging, but since energy harvesting and charging are ignored in typical WSN applications, the impact of the charge/discharge cycle on battery SOH is ignored and set to 1. On the other hand, β was adjusted to report the regions near flat battery plateaus. The average disparity between the MAE scores of SOCs for each iteration was used since they are less sensitive to outliers, whereas RMSE values of V
t at each iteration were used to report any sudden change in voltage values due to environmental or operational conditions (typically resulting from a bad cycle). For each training iteration (epoch ԑ), a forward pass was conducted where the model weights and biases are computed, whereas for each backward pass, the ratio of gradient flow was computed. In this regard, the Adam optimization method was used where the KTuner library utilized random search, and DEAP libraries (De Rainville et al., 2012) was used for Genetic Algorithm based model optimization.

Although these techniques differ in methodology, computing requirements, and integration, both strategies are aimed at maximizing model hyperparameters to produce lower validation loss. The GA method leverages an evolutionary strategy by initializing a population of candidate configurations, where each individual represents a pair of hyperparameters. In this regard, for battery chemistry-specific optimization, the GA optimizer was executed with a population size of N
pop = 10, with a maximum of G = 20 generations, a crossover probability of p
c = 0.6, a per-gene mutation probability of p
m = 0.05, with an overall tournament size of 3, and an elitism rate of e= 2. Furthermore, an early stopping strategy with a patience threshold of 8 epochs was applied to avoid longer convergence and execution times. Early stopping threshold intervals were selected based on (Safe et al., 2004), where termination occurred either when the maximum generation limit was reached or when no improvement in MAE was observed for 8 consecutive generations. It is important to mention that this threshold may vary depending upon a number of factors (such as dataset size, hardware capability, and model architecture, to name a few). A smaller threshold may reduce the execution times; however, it could potentially ignore the prospects of further optimization beyond a certain local corpus. On the other hand, a larger threshold value could potentially result in longer execution time and, in some instances, resource exhaustion. Although the KTuner library that operates on random search differs from the GA-based evolutionary process, it was established that a similar evaluation budget was supplied for direct comparison. Therefore, the optimization was constrained to T = 200 trials, which is equivalent to a GA-based (N
pop = 10, G = 20, therefore 10 ×20 =200 individuals) nominal evaluation budget.

In addition to battery chemistry-specific settings, a unified GA configuration with the least amount of MAE variance was recorded for reproducibility across each dataset. This configuration was used (N
pop = 20, G = 20, p
c = 0.8, p
m = 0.05, and e=2) with similar early stopping threshold and termination criteria, as mentioned above. The unified GA optimizer achieved similar mean error values with marginal variance across battery chemistries and therefore could be used as a reproducible reference point. Algorithm 1 details the pseudocode for this process.

It was observed that the GA-based optimization provides better parameter coverage at each mutation and is more suited to adapt to datasets from various battery types operating at different environmental and operational conditions. However, this requires a large convergence time, requiring substantially larger GPU and CPU support, which are crucial for reducing overall execution times (as discussed in the next section). Algorithm 1
Hyperparameter optimization procedures for the LSTM model (GA and KTuner)
Table
Image 1

Although similar battery capacities and discharge profiles were considered for these experiments, the discharge behavior for each battery chemistry varies slightly with environmental conditions. Furthermore, the battery discharge behavior exhibits significant variations due to different cut-off voltages, particularly across regions or around 20 % of remaining battery capacity. Table 4 presents the overall summary of the recorded features and their statistical relationship. It was observed that the mean variance for Lithium-based battery chemistries around region 2 (50–70 % capacity, SOC) is small, thus providing a flatter region for the overall range of temperature values. Whereas, on the other hand, Ni-MH mean variance increased at higher temperature and greater load current values. Furthermore, the larger mean and standard deviation values for current and SOC values explain the larger spread of the values and outliers (mostly in the case of current values) due to resistive loads and passive elements.

While analyzing the correlation patterns across all battery chemistries, the relationships between the SOC, terminal voltage, and current drawn were observed. The temperature values (T
amb
, T
chamber
, T
surf ) had no direct correlation and exhibited very minor and negligible changes as the controlled environmental conditions were maintained throughout the length of each experiment. Furthermore, since the amount of current drawn was very small, it resulted in no significant change in the battery surface temperatures, as shown in Fig. 7.

Additionally, since the temperature gradients in real-world deployments are not abrupt, a standard temperature profile could be used to estimate its impact on the electrochemical processes and thus the available battery energy. Therefore, these features were dropped to reduce model complexity, which helped in reducing training and inferencing times.

Next, a linear relationship between SOC and capacity with a positive correlation of (r = 1.0) was observed, as shown in Fig. 8. It is however, important to mention that since the Coulomb Counting (Ah) method was used to record the available SOC, the initial battery condition was always considered to be 100 %, which may not be the case since several factors impact the rated battery capacity. Battery pre-conditioning, resting period, quality of materials used, and the sophistication of its construction all contribute towards the accuracy of rated vs. available capacity. However, since the amount of available battery energy after the cut-off voltage threshold is not considered and typically dictates the end of charge for batteries, this small disparity in battery capacity may be neglected for device lifetime estimation, especially for low-power electronics. Therefore, the capacity feature was only used to validate the recorded battery dataset and was not used in model development. Similarly, the terminal voltage (represented by final_voltage) demonstrated a strong positive correlation with SOC across all battery chemistries. This resulted in (r = 0.50, r = 0.75, and r = 0.55) for Ni-MH, Li-Po, and Li-Ion batteries, respectively. The terminal voltage and the subsequent charge differential calculation for each Ah iteration helped to calculate the next available SOC. On the other hand, the measured current flowing through the load for each recording was also analyzed, which showed a weaker correlation, therefore not having a direct impact on the model’s predictive behavior. However, since minor voltage variations were observed, especially around the flatter region (which accounts for almost 80 % of the available battery energy), the change in current values is crucial in identifying unique patterns for prediction. Together with timestamps and moving average values, this resulted in accurately mapping the voltage and SOC values as we move from one region to the next. Therefore, the terminal voltage and current were used as predictor variables, whereas SOC was selected as the target variable.

The model-specific estimation accuracy metrics and optimized hyperparameters are detailed in Table 5 and Table 6, respectively. KTuner with Random Search and early stopping focused on a deterministic approach whereby limiting parameter exhaustion as soon as the model converges. Although this approach significantly reduces model training times, it primarily focuses on discrete parameters that are often calculated in a smaller number of iterations. These results are evident from the training trial indices given in Fig. 9 as well as detailed in Table 6. GA-based optimization, on the other hand, performed an exhaustive parameter search, which is carried forward to the next generation with each mutation considering a new set of parameters representing the best fit. GA combines high dropout rates and various LSTM layers for model regularization, resulting in consistent parameter optimization. This resulted in significant model accuracy, where MAE reduced from 0.51 % to 0.299, and MSE scores reduced from 0.0016 % to 0.000419 % resulting in a 97 % improvement in the next iterations.

The validation losses for both optimization schemes for each battery chemistry are presented in Fig. 9, where model loss functions were computed for each training iteration. Although KTuner based RandomSearch incurs significantly smaller computational overhead, the validation loss values are in close agreement with GA-based tuned models, resulting in consistent model performance on unseen data. GA-based optimization works significantly well for Lithium based battery chemistry due to the non-linear relationships between terminal voltage, current flowing, and the ambient conditions recorded in the training dataset, thus utilizing LSTM’s ability to encode temporal dependencies effectively. Furthermore, GA-based optimization resulted in a mix of parameter configurations, such as increasing units from 32 to 108, while reducing the dropout rate to 0.1, resulting in reduced error propagation in successive predictions, which is crucial for Li-Po cells that display significant hysteresis effects during charge-discharge cycles. The GA-optimized LSTM consistently achieved a lower mean absolute error than the random-search baseline across all three chemistries. For Li-ion cells, the best GA configuration reached a mean MAE of 0.349 ± 0.012, compared with 0.378 ± 0.015 for random search. Li-Po cells exhibited a mean MAE of 0.390 ± 0.018 (GA) versus 0.414 ± 0.021 (KTuner), while Ni-MH cells showed 0.412 ± 0.020 (GA) versus 0.437 ± 0.022 (KTuner). In each case, the observed improvements were statistically significant across five folds and three repeats per condition. These results are consistent with previous evidence that evolutionary optimization reduces hyperparameter search bias and converges toward lower-loss regions in complex, nonconvex search spaces.

However, on the other hand, the performance advantage of GA was achieved at the cost of greater computing demand, reflecting the larger number of fitness evaluations (up to 2000). On the Titan V, average training time per individual was approximately 6.5 min, whereas the RTX 4070 averaged 4.8 min, yielding a total GA runtime of approximately 9–11 h per chemistry when early termination was not triggered. By comparison, KTuner library’s random search completed in less than half this time. However, inference latency remained small, where the GA-optimized model required 2.1 ms (Titan V) and 2.7 ms (RTX 4070) for a single forward pass, demonstrating that deployment constraints are primarily associated with training rather than inference.

The SOC estimation error of the Li-Ion battery was examined at temperatures ranging from 5 °C to 45 °C, where the most variance in SOC estimation was observed at 5 °C. Furthermore, with the temperature increase (batteries achieving thermal equilibrium), the lowest at 45 °C, the SOC estimation error was the smallest. Errors gradually increase at decreasing temperatures, especially below 25 °C, indicating decreased LSTM model accuracy for colder temperatures. This is due to a change in the battery’s internal electrochemical behavior that results in continuous terminal voltage fluctuations. These trends and SOC estimation offsets (residuals) are presented in
Fig. 10 and 11,12.

Li-Po batteries exhibited similar behavior as compared to Li-Ion, where SOC estimation offsets were marginally higher near lower temperatures and vice versa. Furthermore, due to similar chemistries and chemical dynamics, these batteries provide similar discharge zones. It could be concluded that particularly for low-power applications (such as WSN), the responses from Li-Ion and Li-PO are similar, where battery chemistry and form factors only result in associated cost variations.

However, compared to Li-based batteries, the Ni-MH battery's SOC error analysis revealed a steady increase in SOC estimation errors over time, with no noticeable peaks at extremely high or low temperatures. The SOC errors offset, however, were minimal and considered marginal. The electrochemical processes are clearly restricted at lower temperatures, which leads to an increase in internal resistance and a decrease in battery performance. This led to slight adjustments in the calculated values that were observed and were noted in the battery terminal voltages and current readings.

The model optimization complexity directly resulted in notable differences in computational performance on two distinct and capable GPU hardware platforms running on (TensorFlow v2.20.0, CUDA release 13, V13.0.48, Build r13, and compiler 362607280). As highlighted in Table 7, Li-Ion models took 19.8 min on average, whereas it took 245.8 min running on the Titan V GPU for KTuner and GA, respectively. This shows almost 12-fold additional time required for GA's exhaustive mutative search in higher-dimensional parameter space without early stopping. The population-based parameter search provides more detailed parameter configurations, which may be useful for fine-tuning battery-specific or application-specific optimization.

However, the resulting model accuracy gains are less significant compared to the increased computational load. The LSTM base architecture uses a single layer LSTM network followed by a linear output that maps the hidden state to SOC. Since the model architecture remains consistent for all battery types, only the numeric parameters or model weights were tuned, therefore ensuring a consistent computational form. In particular, the input dimension for weight-bearing computations is small (voltage and current; temperature channels were dropped to reduce complexity since they showed negligible variance and correlation in our regime), which bounds both parameter size and per-step computation. However, since each battery chemistry (its dataset) provides a different search space, GA convergence varies for each model. This results in varied optimization times; however, due to similar architecture and computational form, the inference times remain consistent, as presented in Table 7. Although GA-based optimization improved accuracy but it increased training times by roughly an order of magnitude due to many fitness evaluations (population × generations). For instance, on Titan V the total GA runtime per chemistry reached 9–11 h (without early stopping) and around 3–4 h on average (with early stopping). Since GA based optimization only focuses on the forward graph or mutation (keeping the base architecture fixed), the model training cost is amortized offline and does not add to model complexity and therefore, inference times. Furthermore, to enable model scaling for known battery types, we maintain and report a baseline configuration derived from the median of GA optima (units, dropout, L, learning rate, batch). The baseline model performs consistently across chemistries and serves as the default deployment profile. The baseline model also exhibited low MAE variance across all datasets, supporting reproducibility. On the other hand, for application-specific requirements, chemistry-specific GA parameters can be directly embedded when battery type and operational conditions are known.

On the other hand, RTX 4070, with its newer hardware architecture and support for the latest TensorFlow tensors, resulted in substantial performance gains for both optimization techniques. This resulted in almost 20 % training time reductions specifically for the GA algorithm for all Lithium-based battery chemistry. Similarly, the improved memory bandwidth, CUDA cores, and tensor support at each LSTM unit helped to reduce training times for Ni-MH batteries. Furthermore, Table 7 provides insights into average model inferencing times recorded on these architectures, where on average millisecond delays were reported for all SOC estimations. These inferencing times (milliseconds) demonstrate that pre-trained and optimized models can be quantized for edge deployment, where a sub-second response can be achieved. In WSN and IoT applications, SOC estimation is not typically reported in real-time, and energy-aware models only utilize this information after a long while to estimate the availability of remaining battery energy; thus, making sub-second inferencing delays viable.

The LSTM-GA model achieves single-pass inferences in approximately 2–3 ms on desktop-class GPUs (NVIDIA Titan V and RTX 4070), and measured latencies remained sub-second even under conservative CPU-only execution. These millisecond inference times align with common IoT and WSN duty cycles where real-time, realistic SOC calculations are updated in minutes. Modern embedded platforms offer GHz and sub-GHz clock speed processors (for instance, Raspberry Pis); even if their bandwidth limitations are to be considered, the millisecond model inferencing times still suffice for SOC calculations. This is primarily due to the fact that inference dominates on-device costs. On the other hand, model training and optimization, which are resource heavy, thus performed offline. Therefore, trained and optimized models could be further quantized with even lower inferencing times without affecting the model's accuracy or performance. 4.2 details quantization and deployment directions specifically targeting INT8/FP16 based quantized model exports for embedded platforms typically used for edge devices.

These detailed experiments reported substantial improvements as compared to our previous approach that utilized Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) based modelling for SOC estimation. However, several limitations must be acknowledged. First, the discharge experiments were performed on single cells under controlled temperature conditions (using thermal chambers). The real-world temperature impact on battery packs may differ slightly as the temperature variations are not gradual. However, since the current drawn is very small, it has no impact on internal temperature rise and therefore ambient temperature impact (marginal) on battery surfaces can occur. Although cell balancing is not required for typical low-power applications, the combined voltage levels for battery/cell packs (such as Alkaline, Ni-MH, and Ni-CD) may vary, resulting in more frequent voltage fluctuations and susceptibility to early cut-off if any single cell fails or discharges differently. Second, although three repeats per condition were conducted, the dataset remains limited in absolute size compared to large-scale EV drive-cycle repositories, which are typically suited for more advanced deep learning-based architectures aimed at high power applications. Third, SOC ground truth was derived from Coulomb counting with an assumed initial 100 % SOC, introducing systematic error if residual capacity deviated from the nominal capacity. The reported dataset in this study ensured that the terminal voltages were measured after each rest (relaxation period) to accurately record the starting SOC. However, in real-world deployments, measuring terminal voltage and initial SOC is not feasible, which could result in erroneous SOC estimation that propagates into the entire discharge cycle. Mission-critical, high-power, and typical EV applications employ various techniques for accurate SOC estimation; however, for typical low-power low-cost applications (such as WSN deployments), the accuracy vs. cost trade-off is inevitable. Fourth, GA optimization required substantial computational resources, which may limit applicability for rapid retraining or adaptive online deployment. Finally, models were trained separately for each chemistry, and cross-chemistry generalization was partially explored where a GA-based best-fit optimization was adapted. Despite these limitations, the results indicate that GA-tuned LSTM models are suitable for SOC estimation in IoT and WSN devices operating at lower powers. The observed inference times and quantization experiments suggest that deployment on microcontrollers with hardware floating-point support is feasible. From a practical standpoint, sensor calibration, battery priming and relaxation periods, and replicate testing should be considered as essential factors of battery data collection.

In future research work, the following will be considered to further improve the training and inferencing times of LSTM-GA models. First, GPU parallel processing using open-sourced frameworks such as OpenCL and Vulkan Compute will be evaluated to conduct a thorough comparative study by contrasting various CUDA-X GPU-accelerated libraries. This will enable the use of low, and variable power GPU core parallel processing to speed up training times by increasing GPU cores, tensors, and overall memory bandwidth, which is crucial for genetic mutation-based evolutions. Next, hybrid LSTM-transformer architectures will be implemented with deterministic optimization for short-term, and probabilistic and genetic mutation-based optimization for long-term temporal dependencies encoding, resulting in model learning and fine-tuning based on the application-specific scenarios. Finally, adaptive FP8 and FP16 quantization with per-tensor weight and activation scaling techniques and TensorRT based post-training quantization (PTQ) will be investigated for model deployment on various embedded platforms.

The global technological advancements have reached astronomical heights in recent years, where the shift towards decarbonization and electrification is mostly very prominent in EVs domains. Alongside EVs, the use of low-power electronics in the form of WSN, IoT, smartwatches, fitness trackers, and environment monitoring sensors has also seen an exponential adoption rise. Although the need for energy management is more crucial in these devices, marginal advancements in terms of battery life management for low-power devices are observed, especially when compared to EVs or similar domains. Furthermore, with rapid advancements and adoptions in battery technologies, the need for an adaptive and scalable solution to accurately monitor a battery’s state of charge is needed for this domain. In this research a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) based Machine Learning model is presented, which is optimized using a genetic algorithm to accurately estimate SOC for several battery chemistries for real-world operational conditions. A multi-chemistry, low-current dataset was generated under controlled environmental conditions to evaluate SOC estimation for IoT-class devices. Using this corpus, an LSTM tuned with a genetic algorithm achieved statistically significant improvements in accuracy compared with a random-search baseline. Reported MAE values were 0.349 ± 0.012 for Li-ion, 0.390 ± 0.018 for Li-Po, and 0.412 ± 0.020 for Ni-MH cells, each reflecting reductions of 5–8 % relative to baseline tuning. Training cost was higher for GA, but inference latency of ∼2 ms per pass confirmed that deployment on resource-constrained devices is feasible. Furthermore, this research provides a thorough dataset as well as a code pipeline that is capable of adapting to other battery chemistries operating in other operational and environmental conditions. Additionally, LSTM-GA’s ability to adapt and scale without incurring additional inferencing times makes it suitable for embedded platform deployment. The LSTM-GA model effectively encoded long-term temporal dependencies, enabling precise observation of the influence of operational and environmental factors on battery performance. These findings can improve energy management, prolong device life, and decrease maintenance costs in remote and resource-limited IoT and WSN implementations. Future work will include cycle aging for joint SOC–SOH modeling, exploring transfer learning for cross-chemistry generalization, and evaluating field deployments where temperature transients, duty-cycle irregularities, and pack-level balancing may introduce additional complexity. Finally, field trials on several commonly used IoT and WSN embedded platforms will be conducted to validate model robustness under real duty-cycle variations and ambient transients. This will enable its practical implementation on not only WSN and IoT devices but also on architecture-neutral smartwatches and other similar low-power devices that require stringent energy management.

Omer Ali: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamad Khairi Ishak: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Chia Ai Ooi: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Ashraf Bani Ahmad: Validation, Methodology. Khalid Ammar: Writing – review & editing.
